reason: Both answers correctly cover the core legal landscape: CFAA risk mostly defused for logged-out scraping (hiQ/Van Buren), but hiQ ultimately lost on breach-of-contract grounds, plus real exposure from CCPA/CPRA data broker rules, and the practical C&D/litigation pattern with the not-risk-free-just-because-public caveat. Answer A adds useful specificity (Meta v. Bright Data contrast, CA Delete Act/DROP mechanism). Answer B is comparably accurate, adds the concrete detail that hiQ paid damages and had to destroy its data, cites LinkedIn's actual recent lawsuit against Proxycurl/Nubela to substantiate its litigiousness claim with a live example, includes source links, and ends with a crisp diagnostic checklist. Neither has disqualifying errors; B is marginally more useful and grounded in current, concrete precedent.
